narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 97% chance that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high failure probability means the model saw your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to students who typically get approved. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, along with your LSAT score. Most influential was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which is in the bottom tier far below the average of about 5.5; this strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure because lower rankings signal challenges in sustaining academic performance over time. Next, your LSAT score of 25—well under the average of 36.7—reinforced that concern, further increasing the predicted risk of not passing the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also much lower than the typical 5.7, added to this by highlighting early struggles that the model views as a continuing pattern. While your stronger undergrad GPA pulled slightly in a positive direction, it wasn't enough to offset these top concerns, especially when compared to students who succeed on the bar and often have higher deciles and LSATs across the board. For many applicants like you from similar backgrounds, these early academic indicators weigh heavily because they've historically correlated with bar outcomes in the dataset. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward or above the average, while addressing any factors behind those low school deciles—perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional coursework. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience as a Black male student overcoming early hurdles could also help in reapplying or appealing to schools with holistic reviews. With deliberate steps like these, you can shift your profile toward the successful passers the model favors.
